title: The Ultimate Checklist for the Night Before a Big Standardized Test
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You've studied for weeks. You know the material. Now it's the night before the big test. What should you do? Hint: not cramming. Here's the exact checklist to prepare your body and mind for peak performance.

Stop Studying by 8:00 PM

Studying the night before a big test is counterproductive. Your brain needs time to consolidate what you've already learned. Cramming only increases anxiety and reduces sleep. Trust your preparation. Put the books away.

If you absolutely must review something, spend 15 minutes skimming flashcards or your one-page summary. Then stop. Anything more is harmful, not helpful.

Lay Everything Out Tonight

Don't leave anything for the morning. Your morning self is not your most organized self. Lay out:

• Your admission ticket (print it now)
• Photo ID (driver's license or school ID)
• Multiple sharpened pencils (with erasers)
• Approved calculator (with fresh batteries)
• Snacks (granola bar, banana, nuts — no sugary junk)
• Water bottle
• Layers (it might be cold or hot — be ready for both)
• Watch (if phones aren't allowed)

Put everything in your bag by the door. You should be able to grab it and go.

Eat a Real Dinner

Not ramen. Not fast food. A real meal with protein, vegetables, and complex carbs. Think chicken and rice, pasta with meat sauce, or a hearty sandwich. Your brain needs fuel. Processed food won't cut it.

Avoid anything too heavy or greasy. You don't want to feel sluggish or have stomach issues tomorrow.

Get 8-10 Hours of Sleep

This is the single most important thing you can do. A tired brain is a slow brain. You'll read questions more slowly, make careless errors, and struggle to recall information you know.

Set an alarm for 9:00 PM to start winding down. Put your phone in another room. Read a book (not a screen). Take a warm shower. Do whatever helps you fall asleep.

If you're nervous, write down your worries on a piece of paper. Put it in an envelope. Tell yourself you'll deal with it tomorrow. This gets the thoughts out of your head.

Set Multiple Alarms

Set your phone alarm. Set a backup alarm on a different device. Ask a parent to wake you up. Do not oversleep. Give yourself plenty of time to eat breakfast and get to the test center without rushing.

Plan to arrive 15-20 minutes early. Rushing increases stress. Being early gives you time to breathe.

Morning Of: Eat Breakfast

Protein and complex carbs. Eggs and toast. Greek yogurt with granola. Peanut butter sandwich. Avoid sugary cereal, donuts, or pastries. The sugar crash will hit during the test.

Drink water. Not coffee (unless you're a regular coffee drinker). Caffeine can make jittery anxiety worse.

During the Test: Pace Yourself

Answer the easy questions first. Mark the hard ones and come back. Use all your time — don't rush to leave. Check your work if you finish early.

If you feel stuck or panicked, close your eyes for 10 seconds. Take three deep breaths. Then open your eyes and try the next question. You can come back to the hard one later.

You've done the work. You're prepared. Now go show what you know.
